label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's figure out which statement is sarcastic. I need to look at the two options and determine which one uses sarcasm. Option A: "Boy, I sure love it when bigots show up in a thread and start getting nosy" Option B: "Boy, I sure love it when bigots show up in a thread and start getting roasted" Sarcasm typically involves saying the opposite of what you mean in a mocking or ironic way. The key here is to look at the tone and context of the statements. Option A says the speaker "sure love[s]" when bigots show up and start getting "nosy". "Getting nosy" means being inquisitive or prying. If someone says they love when bigots are nosy, it could be genuine, but there's something off here since bigots are generally disliked. However, the sarcasm is not immediately obvious because "getting nosy" is not a strong negative term in this context. It might be a bit of a backhanded compliment or sarcasm, but it's not clear-cut. Option B says the speaker "sure love[s]" when bigots show up and start getting "roasted". "Getting roasted" means to be publicly embarrassed or criticized, often in a humorous way. This phrase is more likely to be sarcastic because the speaker is pretending to love when bigots (who are typically disliked) are being roasted (which is a good thing in most contexts). The sarcasm
